---
title: "pycrn: A Python library for structural and numerical analysis of chemical reaction networks under mass-action kinetics"
shorttitle: "pycrn: Chemical Reaction Network Theory in Python"
author:
  - name: Emilio A. Venegas
    email: emiliovenegas10@gmail.com
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - name: Tecnológico de Monterrey, Escuela de Medicina y Ciencias de la Salud
        address: "Ave. Eugenio Garza Sada 2501 Sur, Col. Tecnológico"
        city: "Monterrey, N.L."
        postal-code: "64700"
        country: "México"
date: today
abstract: |
  Chemical Reaction Network Theory (CRNT), developed by Horn, Jackson, and
  Feinberg, provides parameter-free structural theorems that constrain the
  asymptotic dynamics of mass-action systems irrespective of the numerical
  values of the rate constants. Despite the maturity of the theory, modern
  open-source implementations that combine CRNT structural analysis with
  symbolic ordinary differential equation (ODE) construction and robust
  numerical steady-state finding remain scarce. We present `pycrn`, a pure
  Python library that ingests human-readable reaction strings, builds the
  complex reaction graph, computes the deficiency $\delta = n - \ell - s$ and
  weak reversibility, and decides applicability of the Deficiency Zero
  Theorem (DZT) and Deficiency One Theorem (D1T). For systems satisfying
  these structural conditions, `pycrn` certifies---without any simulation
  whatsoever---existence, uniqueness and (for DZT) asymptotic stability of
  the positive steady state in every stoichiometric compatibility class.
  When the structural theorems do not apply, the library provides symbolic
  mass-action ODEs and Jacobians via SymPy and a hybrid numerical solver
  that combines stiff implicit integration with bound-constrained algebraic
  least-squares to locate both stable and unstable fixed points, including
  Hopf bifurcation centres inaccessible to forward integration. We
  demonstrate the workflow on six benchmarks: a reversible isomerisation,
  the Michaelis--Menten enzyme mechanism, the closed and chemostatted
  Brusselator, a catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) miR-21 biosensor, and the
  Goldbeter--Koshland zero-order ultrasensitivity switch. In each case, the
  CRNT-predicted qualitative behaviour (monostability, oscillation,
  uniqueness) is recovered numerically with a residual below
  $10^{-6}\,\mathrm{M\,s^{-1}}$, and the Goldbeter--Koshland dose-response
  curve agrees with the closed-form quasi-steady-state approximation to
  within $1\%$ over a $400\times$ kinase/phosphatase activity scan.
  `pycrn` is open-source (MIT license) and available at
  <https://github.com/emiliovenegas/pycrn>.
keywords:
  - chemical reaction network theory
  - mass-action kinetics
  - deficiency theorems
  - steady-state analysis
  - bifurcation analysis
  - DNA computing
  - systems biology
  - scientific Python

# ── bioRxiv-style submission metadata ────────────────────────────────────
article-category: "New Results"
subject-area: "Systems Biology"

# ── Required statements for bioRxiv ──────────────────────────────────────
author-approvals: |
  All authors have seen and approved the manuscript. The work reported here
  has not been accepted or published elsewhere.
competing-interests: |
  The author declares no competing financial or non-financial interests.
funding: |
  This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the
  public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
distribution-reuse: |
  CC-BY 4.0 International. The accompanying software is distributed under
  the MIT License.
external-data: |
  Source code, examples, test suite, and reproducibility scripts are openly
  available at <https://github.com/emiliovenegas/pycrn>.

# ── Quarto rendering options ─────────────────────────────────────────────
bibliography: references.bib
link-citations: true
number-sections: true
fontsize: 11pt
geometry:
  - margin=1in
linestretch: 1.25
papersize: letter
mainfont: "Times New Roman"
sansfont: "Arial"
monofont: "Courier New"
linkcolor: NavyBlue
citecolor: NavyBlue
urlcolor: NavyBlue
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    toc: false
    colorlinks: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{unicode-math}
        \setmathfont[
          Path=/home/emiliovenegas/.TinyTeX/texmf-dist/fonts/opentype/public/xits/,
          BoldFont=XITSMath-Bold.otf
        ]{XITSMath-Regular.otf}
        \usepackage{mathtools}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \usepackage{siunitx}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[font=small,labelfont=bf]{caption}
        \usepackage{abstract}
        \renewcommand{\absnamepos}{flushleft}
        \setlength{\absleftindent}{0pt}
        \setlength{\absrightindent}{0pt}
        \renewcommand{\abstractname}{\textbf{Abstract}}
        \usepackage{titlesec}
        \titleformat{\section}{\normalfont\large\bfseries}{\thesection}{1em}{}
        \titleformat{\subsection}{\normalfont\normalsize\bfseries}{\thesubsection}{1em}{}
        \titleformat{\subsubsection}{\normalfont\normalsize\itshape}{\thesubsubsection}{1em}{}
        % ── Custom title block with affiliation (overrides Quarto default) ──
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\maketitle}{%
          \begin{center}%
            {\LARGE \@title \par}%
            \vspace{1.5em}%
            {\large Emilio A.\ Venegas\textsuperscript{1,\,*}\par}%
            \vspace{0.6em}%
            {\small\itshape\textsuperscript{1}Tecnológico de Monterrey, Escuela de Medicina y Ciencias de la Salud,\\
              Ave.\ Eugenio Garza Sada 2501 Sur, Col.\ Tecnológico, Monterrey, N.L.\ 64700, México\par}%
            \vspace{0.5em}%
            {\small\textsuperscript{*}Correspondence: \texttt{emiliovenegas10@gmail.com}\par}%
            \vspace{0.9em}%
            {\@date\par}%
          \end{center}%
          \vspace{0.8em}%
        }
        \makeatother
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  cache: false
---

<!--
  bioRxiv preprint submission front-matter checklist
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Article category:        New Results
  Subject area:            Systems Biology
  Title:                   See above
  External data URL:       https://github.com/emiliovenegas/pycrn
  Abstract:                Included above
  Author approvals:        Stated above
  Competing interests:     None declared
  Authors / ORCID:         Emilio Venegas (corresponding)
  Funder:                  None
  Grant numbers:           N/A
  Distribution / reuse:    CC-BY 4.0 (manuscript), MIT (software)
  Manuscript files:        pycrn_preprint.qmd → renders to PDF via XeLaTeX
  Image files (figures):   michaelis_menten.png       (Fig. 1, §3.2)
                           brusselator_combined.png   (Fig. 2, §3.3)
                           cha_kinetics_species.png   (Fig. 3, §3.4)
                           cha_bifurcation.png        (Fig. 4, §3.4)
                           gk_switch_dose_response.png (Fig. 5, §3.5)
                           cha_reaction_graph.png     (Fig. 6, §3.6)
  Image formats:           PNG (accepted by bioRxiv submission system)
  Fonts:                   Times New Roman (text), XITS Math (math)
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-->

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

The dynamics of a chemical reaction network (CRN) endowed with mass-action
kinetics are governed by polynomial ordinary differential equations whose
behaviour, *a priori*, depends jointly on the network topology and on
$O(10^{0}\text{--}10^{2})$ kinetic parameters that are seldom known to better
than one order of magnitude in living systems. A central insight of
twentieth-century mathematical chemistry, originating in the work of Horn,
Jackson, and Feinberg, is that a substantial portion of the qualitative
behaviour---existence and uniqueness of equilibria, monostability,
impossibility of oscillation---is dictated by *purely structural*
invariants of the reaction graph [@horn1972general; @feinberg1972complex;
@feinberg1987chemical]. The two cornerstones of this body of results are
the Deficiency Zero Theorem [@horn1972general; @feinberg1987chemical] and
the Deficiency One Theorem [@feinberg1995existence], each of which
guarantees properties of the steady-state set that hold uniformly over
*every* assignment of positive rate constants. Modern monographs
[@feinberg2019foundations] and review articles [@angeli2009tutorial]
provide a complete account of the theory, and recent results on the global
attractor conjecture [@anderson2011proof] and structural sources of
robustness [@shinar2010structural] continue to extend its reach.

Notwithstanding the maturity of CRNT, the practical bridge between formal
theorems and computational pipelines remains under-served. The
Chemical Reaction Network Toolbox [@ji2011crnreal] and the
constraint-programming approach of @soliman2012crnpy implement parts of the
deficiency machinery, but neither provides an end-to-end Python interface
that (i) accepts the reaction strings biochemists actually write, (ii)
exposes the mass-action ODEs and Jacobian symbolically through SymPy
[@meurer2017sympy], and (iii) couples the structural analysis to a robust
numerical solver capable of finding both stable and *unstable* fixed
points---the latter being indispensable for the location of Hopf
bifurcation centres in chemostatted, open systems [@prigogine1968symmetry].

In this paper we introduce `pycrn`, a small Python library that fills this
gap. Built on top of the SciPy scientific stack [@virtanen2020scipy;
@harris2020array], SymPy [@meurer2017sympy], and NetworkX
[@hagberg2008exploring], `pycrn` implements:

1. parsing of human-readable reaction strings, canonical-form normalisation
   of rate-constant keys, and construction of the complex reaction graph;
2. exact rational computation of the stoichiometric matrix rank, deficiency
   $\delta$, linkage classes, weak reversibility, and conservation laws as
   non-negative integer left null-space vectors;
3. decision procedures for the Deficiency Zero and Deficiency One Theorems,
   including the per-linkage-class deficiency checks required by D1T
   [@feinberg1995existence; @feinberg2019foundations];
4. symbolic mass-action ODE and Jacobian construction with both symbolic
   and numeric rate constants;
5. a hybrid steady-state solver combining the stiff implicit Radau
   integrator [@hairer1996solving; @hindmarsh2005sundials] with
   bound-constrained algebraic least-squares;
6. a wrapper for chemostatted (open) networks in the sense of
   @polettini2014irreversible, where selected species are held at fixed
   concentrations and folded into effective rate prefactors;
7. log-spaced bifurcation scans of any rate constant and a NetworkX-based
   reaction-graph visualiser.

`pycrn` targets two distinct user communities: (a) systems biologists and
synthetic-biology engineers who want to certify, before any wet-lab work,
that an enzymatic cycle [@ferrell2014ultrasensitivity], a DNA strand-displacement
cascade [@yin2008catalytic; @li2011nonenzymatic], or a metabolic motif
[@ang2014considerations] cannot exhibit pathological bistability or
oscillation; and (b) educators and researchers in chemical reaction
network theory who want a compact, fully open-source reference
implementation against which to develop further structural algorithms.

The remainder of this article is organised as follows. Section
[-@sec-methods] presents the mathematical objects and algorithms
implemented in `pycrn`. Section [-@sec-results] applies the library to
six benchmark networks of increasing complexity: a reversible
isomerisation (DZT applies), Michaelis--Menten (δ = 0 but not weakly
reversible), the closed and chemostatted Brusselator (Hopf-driven limit
cycle), the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) miR-21 biosensor (δ = 1, D1T
applies), and the Goldbeter--Koshland zero-order ultrasensitivity switch
[@goldbeter1981amplified]. Section [-@sec-discussion] places `pycrn` in
the context of existing CRN software and outlines limitations and future
directions.

# Mathematical preliminaries and software architecture {#sec-methods}

## Reactions, complexes, and the stoichiometric matrix

We follow the standard notation of @feinberg2019foundations. Let
$\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, \ldots, S_m\}$ denote the set of $m$ chemical
species and let $\mathcal{C}$ denote a finite set of $n$ *complexes*,
where a complex $y \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}^{m}$ is a non-negative integer
linear combination of species. A *reaction* is an ordered pair of
distinct complexes, written $y \to y'$. A reaction network
$\mathcal{N} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{R})$ consists of the
finite set $\mathcal{R}$ of $r$ reactions on $\mathcal{C}$.

The *stoichiometric matrix* $\mathbf{N} \in \mathbb{Z}^{m \times r}$ has
columns indexed by reactions and rows indexed by species, with
$$
N_{ij} \;=\; y'_j(S_i) - y_j(S_i),
$$ {#eq-stoich}
i.e.\ the net change in copy number of species $i$ effected by reaction
$j$. The *stoichiometric subspace* is $S \coloneqq \operatorname{span}\{y'_j - y_j\}_{j=1}^{r} = \operatorname{Range}(\mathbf{N})$, of
dimension $s \coloneqq \operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N})$. For any
trajectory of the kinetic system, the concentration vector
$\mathbf{c}(t) \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^{m}$ is confined to the affine variety
$\mathbf{c}_0 + S$, the *stoichiometric compatibility class* of the
initial condition $\mathbf{c}_0$.

## Mass-action kinetics

Under the law of mass action [@horn1972general; @michaelis1913kinetik],
the flux of the $j$-th reaction $y_j \to y'_j$ with rate constant
$k_j > 0$ is
$$
v_j(\mathbf{c}) \;=\; k_j\, \mathbf{c}^{y_j}
                  \;\coloneqq\; k_j \prod_{i=1}^{m} c_i^{\,y_j(S_i)},
$$ {#eq-massaction}
and the species-concentration ODE system reads
$$
\dot{\mathbf{c}}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{N}\,\mathbf{v}\bigl(\mathbf{c}(t)\bigr),
\qquad
\mathbf{c}(0) = \mathbf{c}_0 \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^{m}.
$$ {#eq-odes}
The Jacobian along a trajectory is
$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{c}) \;=\; \mathbf{N}\,\bigl[\partial \mathbf{v}/\partial \mathbf{c}\bigr](\mathbf{c}),
\qquad
\frac{\partial v_j}{\partial c_i} \;=\; y_j(S_i)\,\frac{v_j(\mathbf{c})}{c_i},
$$ {#eq-jac}
with the convention that the right-hand side equals
$k_j\,y_j(S_i)\,\prod_{k \neq i} c_k^{y_j(S_k)}$ when $c_i = 0$. In
`pycrn`, equations (@eq-odes)--(@eq-jac) are constructed symbolically as
SymPy expressions via `network.CRNetwork.odes()` and
`network.CRNetwork.jacobian()`; rate constants may be kept symbolic
($k_1, k_2, \ldots$) or substituted by their numeric values.

## Conservation laws

Conservation laws are vectors $\boldsymbol{\ell} \in \mathbb{Z}^{m}_{\ge 0}$
in the *left* null space of $\mathbf{N}$,
$$
\boldsymbol{\ell}^{\!\top}\mathbf{N} \;=\; \mathbf{0},
$$ {#eq-cl}
so that $\boldsymbol{\ell}^{\!\top}\mathbf{c}(t)$ is invariant along every
trajectory. `pycrn` computes a non-negative integer basis of
$\operatorname{LeftNull}(\mathbf{N})$ via exact rational arithmetic with
SymPy, then post-processes the basis (sign normalisation, integer
scaling, and elementary reduction) to recover physical moiety totals. The
algorithm exploits the fact that, for mass-balanced networks, a
non-negative integer basis is always admissible
[@feinberg2019foundations; @rao2016graph].

## Deficiency, linkage classes, and weak reversibility

The *reaction graph* $G = (\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{R})$ has the complexes as
nodes and the reactions as directed edges. A *linkage class* is a weakly
connected component of $G$; we denote their number by $\ell$. The
*deficiency* of the network is
$$
\boxed{\;\delta \;\coloneqq\; n - \ell - s \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\;}
$$ {#eq-deficiency}
where $n = |\mathcal{C}|$, $\ell$ is the number of linkage classes, and
$s = \operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N})$. The non-negativity of $\delta$ is a
classical fact [@feinberg1987chemical]. The network is *weakly reversible*
if every weakly connected component of $G$ is also strongly connected.

## Feinberg's structural theorems

The two principal results implemented in `pycrn` are:

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
**Theorem 1 (Deficiency Zero Theorem; Horn--Jackson 1972, Feinberg 1972).** *Let
$\mathcal{N}$ be a weakly reversible reaction network with $\delta = 0$.
Then for every choice of positive rate constants
$\{k_j\}_{j=1}^{r} \subset \mathbb{R}_{>0}$, the mass-action system
(@eq-odes) admits exactly one positive equilibrium in every positive
stoichiometric compatibility class, and that equilibrium is locally
asymptotically stable. In particular, sustained oscillations and
multistability are impossible* [@horn1972general; @feinberg2019foundations].
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
**Theorem 2 (Deficiency One Theorem; Feinberg 1995).** *Let
$\mathcal{N}$ have deficiency $\delta = 1$ and assume that each linkage
class $L_q$ has per-linkage-class deficiency $\delta_q \in \{0, 1\}$ with
at most one $\delta_q = 1$. Then for every choice of positive rate
constants, the mass-action system (@eq-odes) admits at most one positive
equilibrium in every positive stoichiometric compatibility class*
[@feinberg1995existence; @feinberg2019foundations].
:::

`pycrn` decides the structural hypotheses of both theorems by direct graph
construction (NetworkX), exact rank computation (NumPy
[@harris2020array]), and per-LC deficiency calculation via QR-pivoted
row selection of $\mathbf{N}$ [@virtanen2020scipy]. The decision is
returned in human-readable form by `CRNetwork.crnt_summary()` and
programmatically by the `CRNTResult` dataclass.

## Open (chemostatted) networks

A *chemostatted* species is one whose concentration is held constant by an
external reservoir, mimicking continuous replenishment in a flow reactor
or a buffer with effectively infinite capacity. Following the open-network
formalism of @polettini2014irreversible, `pycrn` removes chemostatted
species from the dynamic state vector and folds their (fixed)
concentrations into *effective* rate constants:
$$
k_j^{\mathrm{eff}} \;=\; k_j \,\prod_{S_i \in \mathrm{chem}}
                            c_i^{\,y_j(S_i)},
$$ {#eq-fold}
so that the reduced ODE system contains only the dynamic species.
Crucially, on open systems the conservation-law left null space is in
general empty and forward integration cannot locate Hopf-unstable fixed
points; `pycrn` therefore switches to a *scale-aware multi-start*
bound-constrained least-squares strategy that retains the ability to find
unstable equilibria.

## Hybrid steady-state solver

For a closed or semi-closed network with $p$ independent conservation
laws encoded as the rows of $\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{Z}^{p \times m}$ and
totals $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^{p}$, the steady-state
problem becomes the constrained polynomial system
$$
\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{ind}}\,\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{c}^\ast) \;=\; \mathbf{0},
\qquad
\mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{c}^\ast \;=\; \boldsymbol{\tau},
\qquad
\mathbf{c}^\ast \;\ge\; \mathbf{0},
$$ {#eq-ss}
where $\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{ind}}$ collects $s$ linearly independent rows
of $\mathbf{N}$ selected by QR pivoting. `pycrn` first attempts forward
integration of (@eq-odes) with SciPy's stiff implicit Radau IIA solver
[@hairer1996solving; @virtanen2020scipy] from the user-supplied initial
condition, then runs bound-constrained algebraic least-squares
(`scipy.optimize.least_squares`, trust-region reflective method) seeded
both at the user IC and at $n_{\mathrm{attempts}} - 2$ random points on
the conservation manifold. For open networks the integration step is
skipped and only the algebraic strategy is used. Duplicate solutions
(relative $\ell_2$ distance below $1\%$) and non-physical solutions are
filtered, after which the Jacobian (@eq-jac) is evaluated at each
candidate and its eigenvalues classified as in @tbl-stability.

| Eigenvalue pattern (significant) | Classification |
|--|--|
| $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda) < 0,\ \operatorname{Im}(\lambda) = 0$ for all $\lambda$ | stable node |
| $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda) < 0$, some $\operatorname{Im}(\lambda) \ne 0$ | stable spiral |
| $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda) > 0$, some $\operatorname{Im}(\lambda) \ne 0$ | **unstable focus** (Hopf candidate) |
| $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda) > 0,\ \operatorname{Im}(\lambda) = 0$ for some $\lambda$ | unstable node / saddle |
| $|\operatorname{Re}(\lambda)| < \varepsilon$ | non-hyperbolic |

: Stability classification implemented in `analysis.classify_steady_state()`. Eigenvalues with $|\lambda| < 10^{-4}\,\max_k |\lambda_k|$ are treated as zero (artefacts of the conservation-law dimensions) and excluded. The `is_oscillatory` flag is set whenever any significant eigenvalue has a non-trivial imaginary part, covering both stable spirals and unstable foci. {#tbl-stability}

# Results {#sec-results}

We illustrate `pycrn`'s end-to-end workflow on six benchmark networks of
increasing complexity. All examples are bundled with the source
distribution (`examples/01–06`).

## Reversible isomerisation: parameter-free uniqueness {#sec-rev}

The simplest non-trivial network is the reversible isomerisation
$A \rightleftharpoons B$. There are $n = 2$ complexes, $\ell = 1$ linkage
class, $\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N}) = 1$, hence $\delta = 0$. The
network is weakly reversible, so the Deficiency Zero Theorem applies; for
any positive forward and reverse rate constants $k_f, k_r$ the unique
positive equilibrium consistent with the conservation law $A + B = T$ is
$$
A^\ast \;=\; \frac{k_r}{k_f + k_r}\,T, \qquad
B^\ast \;=\; \frac{k_f}{k_f + k_r}\,T,
$$ {#eq-aoffb}
and it is asymptotically stable. `pycrn` recovers
(@eq-aoffb) numerically to machine precision: for
$k_f = 1.0$, $k_r = 0.5$, $A_0 = 2$, $B_0 = 0$ the solver returns
$A^\ast = 0.6\overline{6}$ and $B^\ast = 1.\overline{3}$ with residual
$\|\mathbf{N}\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{c}^\ast)\|_2 < 10^{-12}\,\mathrm{M\,s^{-1}}$
and eigenvalues $\{0, -1.5\}$. The single zero eigenvalue is the expected
signature of the conservation law dimension.

```python
from pycrn import CRNetwork
rn = CRNetwork.from_string(
    ["A <-> B"],
    rates={"A -> B": 1.0, "B -> A": 0.5},
)
print(rn.crnt_summary())          # δ = 0, DZT applies
ss = rn.steady_states({"A": 2.0, "B": 0.0})[0]
# ss.concentrations  ≈ {'A': 0.6667, 'B': 1.3333}
# ss.is_stable       == True
```

## Michaelis--Menten: $\delta = 0$ but not weakly reversible {#sec-mm}

The classical enzyme mechanism [@michaelis1913kinetik]
$$
E + S \;\rightleftharpoons\; ES \;\to\; E + P
$$ {#eq-mm}
has three complexes, one linkage class, and
$\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N}) = 2$, hence $\delta = 0$. However the
network is *not* weakly reversible because the catalytic step
$ES \to E + P$ has no reverse edge in the complex graph. The Deficiency
Zero Theorem therefore *does not* apply in its stability form. `pycrn`
correctly reports this and returns the two conservation laws
$E + ES = E_{\mathrm{tot}}$ and $S + ES + P = S_{\mathrm{tot}}$ as
non-negative integer left null-space vectors. For the standard parameters
$k_f = 10^{6}\,\mathrm{M^{-1}s^{-1}}$,
$k_r = 10^{3}\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, $k_{\mathrm{cat}} = 100\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$
and initial conditions $E_0 = 1\,\mu\mathrm{M}$, $S_0 = 100\,\mu\mathrm{M}$,
the numerical equilibrium satisfies enzyme conservation
$E^\ast + ES^\ast = E_0$ to within machine precision, and the
quasi-steady-state estimate
$$
[ES]_{\mathrm{QSS}} \;=\; \frac{E_0\,[S]^\ast}{K_M + [S]^\ast},
\qquad
K_M \;=\; \frac{k_r + k_{\mathrm{cat}}}{k_f}
$$ {#eq-qss}
tracks the full mass-action concentration $[ES](t)$ obtained by `pycrn`
to better than $0.25\%$ throughout the catalytic phase
(Fig.\ [-@fig-mm]). At the true mathematical steady state the irreversible
catalytic step drives all substrate to product, so $[ES]^\ast = 0$;
the practical relevance of the QSS approximation is therefore as a
*dynamic* tracker during enzyme turnover rather than as a steady-state
identity.

![Michaelis--Menten enzyme kinetics. (a) Full mass-action time course
($E_0 = 1\,\mu\mathrm{M}$, $S_0 = 100\,\mu\mathrm{M}$) on a twin-axis plot:
$S$ and $P$ on the left axis, $E$ and $ES$ on the right. (b) Comparison of
the full mass-action $[ES](t)$ (solid) with the quasi-steady-state
approximation $E_0\,[S](t)/(K_M+[S](t))$ (dashed); the two curves overlap
to within $0.25\%$ after the initial $\sim 5\,\mathrm{ms}$ transient.
Generated by `examples/02_michaelis_menten.py`.](../examples/michaelis_menten.png){#fig-mm width=90%}

## Brusselator: Hopf bifurcation under chemostatting {#sec-bruss}

The four-step Brusselator [@prigogine1968symmetry]
$$
A \xrightarrow{k_1} X, \qquad
B + X \xrightarrow{k_2} Y + D, \qquad
2X + Y \xrightarrow{k_3} 3X, \qquad
X \xrightarrow{k_4} E
$$ {#eq-bruss-reactions}
is the canonical example of a chemical oscillator. With *all* six species
treated as dynamic, `pycrn` reports $\delta = 0$ but the network is not
weakly reversible (no autocatalytic complex appears as a product of any
other reaction), so neither Feinberg theorem certifies global asymptotic
stability. Forward integration of the full closed system converges to a
fixed point because $A$ and $B$ deplete; this is the well-known
distinction between the *closed* and the *classical chemostatted*
Brusselator [@prigogine1968symmetry].

Holding $A$ and $B$ at constant concentrations $A = 1$, $B = 3$ via the
`chemostatted=` argument folds their values into effective rate
constants (@eq-fold) and reduces the system to the standard two-variable
form
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{X} &\;=\; A - (B+1)\,X + X^{2}\,Y,\\
\dot{Y} &\;=\; B\,X - X^{2}\,Y,
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-bruss-2d}
with unique fixed point $X^\ast = A$, $Y^\ast = B/A$. Linearisation
yields the Jacobian
$$
\mathbf{J}(X^\ast, Y^\ast)
\;=\;
\begin{pmatrix} B - 1 & A^{2} \\[2pt] -B & -A^{2} \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{J} \;=\; B - 1 - A^{2},
\qquad
\det\mathbf{J} \;=\; A^{2},
$$ {#eq-bruss-jac}
so that a supercritical Hopf bifurcation occurs at the threshold
$B = 1 + A^{2}$ [@prigogine1968symmetry]. For $A = 1$, $B = 3$ the
condition $B > 1 + A^{2}$ is satisfied and `pycrn` locates the
*unstable* fixed point $(X^\ast, Y^\ast) = (1, 3)$ algebraically (forward
integration would orbit the limit cycle indefinitely); the Jacobian
eigenvalues are $\lambda_{\pm} = 1 \pm i\sqrt{3} - 1 = 1 \pm i\,\sqrt{3}$
with positive real part, correctly classifying the fixed point as an
unstable focus. For $B = 1.5 < 1 + A^{2}$ the same fixed point becomes a
stable spiral.

![Brusselator dynamics with $A$ and $B$ chemostatted. Top row ($B = 3$,
oscillatory): (a) time series of $X$ and $Y$; (b) phase portrait showing
the transient (grey) relaxing onto the limit cycle (green) that orbits the
algebraic fixed point $(X^\ast, Y^\ast) = (1, 3)$, classified as an
unstable focus by `pycrn`. Bottom row ($B = 1.5$, damped):
(c) time series; (d) phase portrait spiralling into the stable equilibrium
$(1, 1.5)$. The black markers indicate the equilibria returned by
`pycrn`'s algebraic solver. Generated by
`examples/05_brusselator_chemostatted.py`.](../examples/brusselator_combined.png){#fig-bruss width=95%}

## CHA miR-21 biosensor: parameter-free uniqueness via D1T {#sec-cha}

DNA strand-displacement networks have emerged as a versatile platform for
constructing programmable molecular sensors [@yin2008catalytic;
@li2011nonenzymatic]. The catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) circuit for the
microRNA biomarker miR-21 comprises four reversible reactions:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{miR21} + H_{1} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; \mathrm{miR21}\!:\!H_{1},
   && \text{(toehold binding)}\\
\mathrm{miR21}\!:\!H_{1} + H_{2} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; H_{1}\!:\!H_{2} + \mathrm{miR21},
   && \text{(strand displacement, catalyst release)}\\
H_{1}\!:\!H_{2} + \mathrm{CP} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; H_{1}\!:\!H_{2}\!:\!\mathrm{CP},
   && \text{(capture-probe reporter)}\\
H_{1} + H_{2} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; H_{1}\!:\!H_{2}.
   && \text{(background leakage)}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-cha}
Forward and reverse rate constants were obtained from NUPACK
thermodynamic calculations [@zadeh2011nupack] at 37 °C in
$137\,\mathrm{mM}$ NaCl, $10\,\mathrm{mM}$ MgCl$_2$. `pycrn`'s structural
analysis returns $m = 7$ species, $n = 8$ complexes, $\ell = 4$ linkage
classes, $\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N}) = 3$, hence
$$
\delta_{\mathrm{CHA}} \;=\; 8 - 4 - 3 \;=\; 1.
$$ {#eq-cha-def}
The network is weakly reversible and each linkage class has
per-LC deficiency $\delta_q \in \{0, 1\}$ with exactly one $\delta_q = 1$,
so the Deficiency One Theorem applies and certifies *at most one* positive
equilibrium per stoichiometric compatibility class. `pycrn` further
returns the four moiety totals (miR21$_{\mathrm{tot}}$, $H_{1,\mathrm{tot}}$,
$H_{2,\mathrm{tot}}$, $\mathrm{CP}_{\mathrm{tot}}$) as a non-negative
integer basis of $\operatorname{LeftNull}(\mathbf{N})$.

With $H_{1,0} = H_{2,0} = \mathrm{CP}_0 = 100\,\mathrm{nM}$ and
$[\mathrm{miR21}]_0 = 10\,\mathrm{nM}$, mass-action integration of the
network (Fig.\ [-@fig-cha-kin]) reveals the characteristic catalytic
turnover: miR-21 is rapidly consumed into the miR21$\cdot$H$_1$ complex,
which is then displaced by H$_2$ to regenerate free miR-21 and form the
H$_1\!:\!H_2$ dimer that binds the capture probe. The hybrid solver
returns the unique equilibrium
$[H_{1}\!:\!H_{2}\!:\!\mathrm{CP}]^\ast = 0.34\,\mathrm{nM}$, with
$\operatorname{Re}\lambda < 0$ for all significant eigenvalues
($\operatorname{Re}\lambda_{\max} \approx -3 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$).
A log-spaced scan over $[\mathrm{miR21}]_0 \in [0.1, 100]\,\mathrm{nM}$
(Fig.\ [-@fig-cha]) confirms the D1T prediction empirically: the
dose-response curve is single-valued and monotone, with no hint of
hysteresis or alternative branches.

![Mass-action kinetics of the CHA cascade at four miR-21 trigger
concentrations (10 nM, 1 nM, 100 pM, and no-trigger leakage-only control).
The reporter complex $H_{1}\!:\!H_{2}\!:\!\mathrm{CP}$ (dashed black) is
the electrochemical output; H$_1$ and H$_2$ are the fuel hairpins held at
$100\,\mathrm{nM}$. The leakage-only panel demonstrates that, at the rate
constants returned by NUPACK, spontaneous H$_1\!:\!H_2$ dimerisation is
negligible over a $120\,\mathrm{min}$
window.](../examples/cha_kinetics_species.png){#fig-cha-kin width=95%}

![Steady-state reporter signal $[H_{1}\!:\!H_{2}\!:\!\mathrm{CP}]^\ast$
as a function of the initial miR-21 concentration. The single-valued,
monotone trace is consistent with the parameter-free uniqueness guarantee
of the Deficiency One Theorem ($\delta_{\mathrm{CHA}} = 1$). Generated by
`examples/04_cha_cascade.py`.](../examples/cha_bifurcation.png){#fig-cha width=70%}

## Goldbeter--Koshland switch: ultrasensitivity without bistability {#sec-gk}

The Goldbeter--Koshland (GK) covalent-modification cycle
[@goldbeter1981amplified; @ferrell2014ultrasensitivity] is the canonical
mechanism for *zero-order ultrasensitivity*. The full mass-action
mechanism couples a kinase arm and a phosphatase arm,
$$
\begin{aligned}
W + E_{1} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; W\!:\!E_{1} \;\to\; W_{p} + E_{1},\\
W_{p} + E_{2} &\;\rightleftharpoons\; W_{p}\!:\!E_{2} \;\to\; W + E_{2},
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-gk}
and possesses $m = 6$ species, $n = 6$ complexes, $\ell = 2$ linkage
classes, $\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{N}) = 3$, so that
$\delta_{\mathrm{GK}} = 6 - 2 - 3 = 1$. The network is *not* weakly
reversible (the catalytic steps are irreversible), and each linkage class
has per-LC deficiency 0 or 1 with at most one equal to 1: the Deficiency
One Theorem therefore applies. The structural conclusion is that for
*any* positive choice of $k_{\mathrm{on}}, k_{\mathrm{off}},
k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{1}, k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{2}$ and *any* enzyme totals
$E_{1}^{\mathrm{tot}}$, $E_{2}^{\mathrm{tot}}$, the mass-action system
admits at most one positive equilibrium per stoichiometric compatibility
class: **bistability is structurally impossible**.

The Goldbeter--Koshland quasi-steady-state (QSS) approximation
[@goldbeter1981amplified] predicts the fractional activation
$y \coloneqq [W_{p}]^\ast / W^{\mathrm{tot}}$ as the unique root in
$(0, 1)$ of
$$
(1 - y)\bigl(\widetilde{K} + y\bigr)
\;=\;
\frac{1}{u}\,y\bigl(\widetilde{K} + 1 - y\bigr),
\qquad
u \coloneqq \frac{k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{1}\,E_{1}^{\mathrm{tot}}}
                 {k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{2}\,E_{2}^{\mathrm{tot}}},
\qquad
\widetilde{K} \coloneqq \frac{K_M}{W^{\mathrm{tot}}},
$$ {#eq-gk-qss}
with $K_M = (k_{\mathrm{off}} + k_{\mathrm{cat}})/k_{\mathrm{on}}$. In the
deep zero-order regime $W^{\mathrm{tot}} / K_M \gg 1$, the curve
$y(u)$ becomes effectively step-like and the Hill coefficient
$n_H = \log(81)/\log(\mathrm{EC}_{90}/\mathrm{EC}_{10})$ diverges as
$K_M \to 0$. We use $W^{\mathrm{tot}} = 1\,\mathrm{M}$,
$E_{1}^{\mathrm{tot}} = E_{2}^{\mathrm{tot}} = 0.01\,\mathrm{M}$,
$k_{\mathrm{on}} = 10^{4}\,\mathrm{M^{-1}\,s^{-1}}$,
$k_{\mathrm{off}} = 0.1\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$,
$k_{\mathrm{cat}} = 1\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, giving
$W^{\mathrm{tot}}/K_M \approx 9000$. Scanning
$u = k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{1}/k_{\mathrm{cat}}^{2}$ over more than two
decades, `pycrn` finds exactly one steady state at every point
(Fig.\ [-@fig-gk]), confirming the D1T prediction. The numerical
fractional activation agrees with (@eq-gk-qss) to better than $1\%$
across the full scan, and the effective Hill coefficient at half-maximum
exceeds $2 \times 10^{3}$, illustrating the digital character of the
switch.

![Goldbeter--Koshland zero-order ultrasensitivity switch. Left:
fractional activation $y = [W_p]^\ast/W^{\mathrm{tot}}$ from `pycrn`'s
full mass-action solution (markers) overlaid on the QSS prediction
(@eq-gk-qss) (dashed). Right: the same data plotted alone, with the
effective Hill coefficient annotated. The Deficiency One Theorem
guarantees the single-valued nature of the curve. Generated by
`examples/06_goldbeter_koshland.py`.](../examples/gk_switch_dose_response.png){#fig-gk width=95%}

## Reaction graph visualisation {#sec-graph}

For pedagogical exposition and debugging, `pycrn` exposes a NetworkX-based
graph layout via `CRNetwork.draw()`. Figure [-@fig-graph] shows the
complex reaction graph for the CHA network of §[-@sec-cha]; nodes are
complexes, directed edges are reactions, and the four weakly connected
components correspond to the four linkage classes $\ell = 4$ that enter
the deficiency formula (@eq-deficiency).

![Complex reaction graph for the CHA miR-21 biosensor; nodes are complexes
(e.g.\ $\mathrm{miR21}+H_{1}$), directed edges are reactions. Four weakly
connected components ($\ell = 4$) and eight complexes ($n = 8$) on a
stoichiometric subspace of dimension $s = 3$ give
$\delta = 8-4-3=1$.](../examples/cha_reaction_graph.png){#fig-graph width=80%}

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

We have presented `pycrn`, a lightweight Python implementation of
Feinberg-style chemical reaction network theory tightly integrated with
SymPy [@meurer2017sympy] for symbolic ODE construction and SciPy
[@virtanen2020scipy] for numerical equilibration. The combination of
parameter-free structural certification (DZT, D1T) with a hybrid
integration/algebraic solver capable of locating both stable and unstable
fixed points addresses a long-standing gap between the mathematical
theory and applied practice: practitioners can now state, programmatically
and prior to any wet-lab experiment, whether a given network is
*structurally* prohibited from exhibiting bistability or oscillation, and
then back-test that prediction across the whole physically plausible
parameter regime by way of (@eq-odes) and (@eq-ss). The six benchmarks of
§[-@sec-results] exercise the principal capabilities of the library: in
all cases the CRNT-predicted qualitative behaviour is recovered with a
solver residual below $10^{-6}\,\mathrm{M\,s^{-1}}$, and the
Goldbeter--Koshland dose-response curve agrees with the closed-form QSS
approximation to within $1\%$ over the entire $400\times$ activity scan.

Compared with the foundational Chemical Reaction Network Toolbox
[@ji2011crnreal] and the constraint-programming approach of
@soliman2012crnpy, `pycrn` offers (i) an idiomatic Python API tailored to
the modern scientific stack [@harris2020array; @virtanen2020scipy], (ii)
first-class support for chemostatted (open) networks in the spirit of
@polettini2014irreversible---including the algebraic location of
Hopf-unstable fixed points that forward integration cannot reach---and
(iii) symbolic ODE/Jacobian expressions ready for downstream pipelines
such as parameter inference, sensitivity analysis, or thermodynamic
constraint checking [@ederer2007thermodynamically].

Several limitations remain. The current solver does not implement higher
deficiency theorems (e.g.\ deficiency-two or higher-deficiency results)
nor the recent toric-balanced equilibria framework of @craciun2009toric;
the global attractor conjecture [@anderson2011proof] is not invoked.
Bifurcation scans are presently limited to one rate constant at a time,
and continuation-style tracking of unstable branches across folds, as
provided by XPPAUT [@ermentrout2002simulating] or AUTO, is not (yet)
implemented. Model-reduction techniques [@rao2018identifying] and
robustness analysis [@shinar2010structural] are natural next steps, as is
integration with SBML import/export.

In conclusion, `pycrn` provides an accessible, well-tested, and
mathematically rigorous entry point into chemical reaction network theory
for the broader Python scientific community. By coupling Feinberg's
structural theorems to symbolic and numerical tools, it lowers the
barrier between formal CRNT and practical model analysis, with
applications spanning DNA computing [@yin2008catalytic; @zadeh2011nupack],
enzymatic signal transduction [@goldbeter1981amplified;
@ferrell2014ultrasensitivity], and synthetic-circuit design
[@ang2014considerations].

# Data and code availability {.unnumbered}

All source code, examples, the 93-test pytest suite, and scripts to
regenerate every figure in this paper are openly available under the MIT
License at <https://github.com/emiliovenegas/pycrn>. The version used to
prepare this manuscript is tagged `v0.1.0`.

# Author contributions {.unnumbered}

E.V.\ designed the library, implemented the code, performed all numerical
experiments, and wrote the manuscript.

# Acknowledgements {.unnumbered}

The author acknowledges the open-source scientific Python ecosystem
[@harris2020array; @virtanen2020scipy; @meurer2017sympy;
@hagberg2008exploring] without which this work would not have been
possible.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
